sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Each action moves the white character 3 cells in that direction. - White char now at rows 14-28, cols 14-28. Red feet at rows 29-31. - Green frame at rows 29-49, cols 41-49 with interior at ~rows 32-46, cols 44-46. - Need to move character toward green frame: ~9 RIGHT and ~5 DOWN from current position.
- Goal model: Move the white character to interact with the green frame (possibly enter it or match its position).
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT likely move the white character. Need to probe.
- Plan: Batch RIGHT moves to approach the green frame horizontally, then DOWN to align vertically.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 33 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The character has rotated/flipped again. Red blocks are now on the right side. Let me check green count.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green total: {sum(n['pixels'] for n in greens)} in {len(greens)} pieces")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'S':
        minr = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxr = max(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        minc = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxc = max(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        print(f"id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={minr}-{maxr} cols={minc}-{maxc}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Green went from 72 to 108 - it increased! So pressing SPACE again increased green. This is the oscillating pattern I noticed before.
Let me reconsider the whole approach. The pattern seems to be:
- SPACE toggles between two states of the character
- When in one state, SPACE reduces green; in the other state, SPACE increases green
- Moving the character changes the overlap, which affects how much green is reduced/increased
Maybe I need to track the "phase" of SPACE presses. Let me think about this differently.
Looking at the history of green counts:
144 -> 135 -> 99 -> 54 -> 90 -> 99 -> 72 -> 90 -> 45 -> 90 -> 72 -> 108
The reductions happen when:
- Moving character into green frame (overlap increases)
- SPACE at certain positions
The increases happen when:
- Moving character away from green frame
- SPACE at certain positions
Maybe the goal isn't to reduce green to 0. Maybe it's to achieve a specific configuration where the character and green frame form a complete pattern.
Looking at the image, the character is an inverted U-shape and the green is a U-shape. When combined, they might form a rectangle or other complete shape.
Let me try a different approach: instead of randomly pressing SPACE, let me systematically try to position the character so it perfectly overlaps with the green frame, then press SPACE once.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The green frame is a "U" shape (open at bottom). The character is also a U-shape but inverted. When the character is positioned inside the green frame, the overlapping parts might "cancel out" or "fill in" the green.
Looking at the current image, the character is rotated 90 degrees clockwise (red on right). The green frame is below and to the left.
Let me try to get back to a state where the character is upright and positioned to overlap the green frame. I'll try DOWN moves to reposition.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Green went from 72 to 108 - SPACE increased it this time
# The pattern oscillates. Let me try a different approach.
# Maybe I need to move the character DOWN to cover the bottom green piece, then SPACE
# Current char: rows 26-40, cols 38-52
# Green: rows 29-37 (left), 32-34 (right), 38-49 (bottom)
# Let me try DOWN to cover bottom green more
action(['DOWN','DOWN','SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
